study.journal: Ferrando C et al. Individualised perioperative open-lung approach versus standard protective ventilation in abdominal surgery (iPROVE): a randomised controlled trial. Lancet Respir Med. 2018.

population_context.population_description: Patients aged 18 years or older scheduled for abdominal surgery with expected duration longer than 2 hours, intermediate-to-high risk of postoperative pulmonary complications, and body-mass index less than 35 kg/m².
population_context.risk_group: Intermediate-to-high risk for postoperative pulmonary complications undergoing major abdominal surgery
population_context.exclusions: Age <18 years, BMI ≥35 kg/m², low-risk patients, expected surgical duration <2 hours

intervention.name: Individualised perioperative open-lung approach (OLA)
components: Individualised PEEP based on respiratory system dynamic compliance (Cdyn)
Individualised postoperative continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) or standard postoperative oxygen therapy
intervention.parameter_values: Low tidal volume plus PEEP; PEEP individualised after lung recruitment manoeuvre to optimise Cdyn

comparator.description: Standard intraoperative lung-protective ventilation plus standard postoperative oxygen therapy (STD–O)

outcome_effect.outcome_name: Composite of pulmonary and systemic complications during the first 7 postoperative days

strengths: Large multicentre prospective randomised controlled trial across 21 teaching hospitals
Four-arm design allowing comparison of multiple individualised and standard strategies
Masking of patients after 24 hours with independent data assessment
First study to continuously individualise ventilatory strategy throughout intraoperative and postoperative period
limitations: No significant differences between groups despite hypothesis of benefit
All enrolled patients had healthy lungs at baseline
Spanish teaching hospitals only; limited generalisability
Investigators not masked in operating and immediate postoperative rooms
Intermediate-to-high risk patients selected; results may not apply to low-risk patients
Intraoperative PEEP increased in only 14% of standard groups due to hypoxaemia

applicability.generalizability_notes: Findings apply to patients with healthy lungs undergoing major abdominal surgery with intermediate-to-high risk for postoperative complications. Results may not generalise to critically ill patients, patients with severe lung disease, those with BMI ≥35 kg/m², or low-risk surgical patients. Spanish healthcare setting may limit international applicability.

quoted_sentences.design: We did this prospective, multicentre, randomised controlled trial in 21 teaching hospitals in Spain. Patients were randomly assigned (1:1:1:1) online to receive one of four lung-protective ventilation strategies using low tidal volume plus positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP).
quoted_sentences.intervention: Patients were randomly assigned (1:1:1:1) online to receive one of four lung-protective ventilation strategies using low tidal volume plus positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP): open-lung approach (OLA)–iCPAP (individualised intraoperative ventilation [individualised PEEP after a lung recruitment manoeuvre] plus individualised postoperative continuous positive airway pressure [CPAP]), OLA–CPAP (intraoperative individualised ventilation plus postoperative CPAP), STD–CPAP (standard intraoperative ventilation plus postoperative CPAP), or STD–O (standard intraoperative ventilation plus standard postoperative oxygen therapy).
quoted_sentences.results: Risk of pulmonary and systemic complications did not differ for patients in OLA–iCPAP (110 [46%] of 241, relative risk 0·89 [95% CI 0·74–1·07; p=0·25]), OLA–CPAP (111 [47%] of 238, 0·91 [0·76–1·09; p=0·35]), or STD–CPAP groups (118 [48%] of 244, 0·95 [0·80–1·14; p=0·65]) when compared with patients in the STD–O 2 group (125 [51%] of 244).
quoted_sentences.outcome: In patients who have major abdominal surgery, the different perioperative open lung approaches tested in this study did not reduce the risk of postoperative complications when compared with standard lung-protective mechanical ventilation.
